# Persistent Sessions

Chapter 13 left one hazard unsolved. Over `ssh`, the connection
is a lifeline: close the laptop or lose the network and the
remote shell receives `SIGHUP`, and by default the job dies with
it. Chapter 9 named `nohup` as the stopgap, a way to keep a job
alive through a disconnect, but a limited one, since you cannot
reattach to the job and drive it, only follow its log from the
outside. Both chapters pointed here for the real answer.

That answer is `tmux`, a terminal multiplexer. The idea behind
it is one sentence: the connection is disposable, the session is
not. `tmux` runs a program that owns a session living on the
machine where `tmux` runs, holding your shells and their running
jobs. You *attach* a terminal to that session to watch and type,
and *detach* to walk away; the session, and everything running
in it, stays put. Detach on purpose, or get cut off by a dropped
network, and the difference is the same: nothing. You reconnect
later, from anywhere, reattach, and the job is still running,
right where you left it, its recent output still on screen. On a
remote server this is the difference between a job that survives
the night and one that dies when your laptop sleeps.

This is a lean chapter on purpose. It teaches the small core a
researcher actually needs, the session loop, the
session/window/pane structure, and the survive-a-disconnect
payoff, and deliberately skips tmux's large surface of
copy-mode, theming, status-line customization, and plugins. When
in doubt, this chapter cuts. `tmux` builds on Chapter 2's process
model (a session is a set of processes that keep running whether
or not a terminal is attached) and does not re-teach it; it also
does not re-teach `ssh` (Chapter 13), the run log (Chapter 10),
or `nohup`/`SIGHUP` (Chapter 9), all of which it leans on by
cross-reference.

A note on where the output comes from. The core loop, the
session structure, `send-keys`/`capture-pane`, and the
"progressed while detached" demo all run in this book's Linux
sandbox (tmux 3.2a), captured non-interactively: an interactive
`tmux attach` takes over the terminal and would hang a scripted
capture, the Chapter 9 lesson, so the captures drive `tmux`
through its command interface (`new-session -d`, `ls`,
`capture-pane`, `send-keys`) and never attach. The one thing the
sandbox cannot show, that a session outlives the `ssh`
connection that started it, is captured on the author's Mac
against a real remote Linux server by
`transcripts/capture-ch14-mac.sh`. Because a remote session's
prompt, `tmux ls`, and status line all expose identifying
values, the captures mask, at capture time, the account
(`[account]`), the hostname (`[hostname]`), and any address
(`[ip]`); the server alias is shown as `lab-server` and every
session name (`job`, `work`, `analysis`, `build`) is one the
book chose, not a real one.

## Sessions that outlive the connection {.tier-beginner}

The whole tool reduces to a short loop: start a session, detach
from it, list what is running, reattach, and eventually end it.
Interactively you start one by name with `tmux new -s work`,
which drops you into a shell inside the session. To leave it
running and return to your plain terminal, you press the *prefix
key* `Ctrl-b`, release it, then press `d` (for detach). tmux's
default *key bindings* all have this shape: press `Ctrl-b`,
release, then another key. The prefix exists so tmux's own keys
never collide with the keys your programs use; in the default
bindings `Ctrl-b d` means "detach" and `Ctrl-b c` means "new
window". (The `tmux ...` commands you type at the shell, below,
are separate from these bindings and need no prefix.)

Because an interactive session cannot be scripted (attaching
seizes the terminal), the captures here use the non-interactive
equivalents, which do exactly the same thing without a keyboard.
`tmux new-session -d -s work` creates a session named `work` and
immediately detaches (`-d`); `tmux ls` lists the sessions the
server is holding; `tmux kill-session -t work` ends one by name:

```bash
$ tmux new-session -d -s work -x 80 -y 24
$ tmux ls
work: 1 windows (created Sun Jul  5 21:11:43 2026)
$ tmux kill-session -t work
$ tmux ls
no server running on /tmp/tmux-1187/default
```

The `-s work` names the session so you can find it again; the
`-x 80 -y 24` fixes its size (useful only for reproducible
captures, an interactive session takes your terminal's size).
After the session is created, `tmux ls` shows it exists with no
terminal attached, and once killed, the last session gone, the
tmux server itself exits. The everyday loop is the interactive
mirror of this: `tmux new -s work`, do your work, `Ctrl-b d` to
detach, `tmux attach -t work` to come back.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
Your connection drops mid-job, or you close the laptop by
accident. Nothing is lost, because the job was in a tmux session
on the server, not tied to your terminal. Reconnect with `ssh`,
run `tmux ls` to confirm the session is still listed, and
`tmux attach -t job` to rejoin it. With no argument `tmux attach`
joins your most recent session, but naming it with `-t` is the
habit that scales once you keep more than one. If another client
is still attached to that session (a desktop you left at the
office), tmux by default lets both attach at once and share the
view; `tmux attach -d -t job` detaches that other client as you
join, so the session is yours alone.
:::

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The prefix key is also the classic confusion, because tmux
happily runs inside tmux. `ssh` into a server and start tmux
there while a local tmux is already running, and two servers are
stacked; the prefix goes to the *outer*, local one. `Ctrl-b c`
then opens a window in your laptop's session, not the server's,
and it looks like the server's tmux is ignoring you. To send the
prefix through to the inner session, press it twice: `Ctrl-b
Ctrl-b c`. The simpler habit is to not nest at all: on a remote
box use the server's tmux and leave your local terminal plain,
so there is only ever one prefix to think about.
:::

## Sessions, windows, and panes {.tier-beginner}

A session is not flat. It contains *windows* (like tabs, one
full screen each), and a window can be split into *panes* (tiled
regions of that screen). In normal use the nesting goes one way:
a server holds sessions, a session holds windows, a window holds
panes, and detaching or reattaching moves the whole tree at once. For
the running example, one session can hold everything a single
analysis needs:

```text
session "analysis"        # survives your disconnects
|-- window 0: shell       # editing, a REPL, ad-hoc commands
`-- window 1: "build"     # the long job lives here
    |-- pane 0            # the job, running
    `-- pane 1            # a tail -f on its log
```

That map is a schematic; tmux reports the real structure with
`list-windows` and `list-panes`. Building the same shape without
attaching, `new-window` adds window 1 and `split-window -v`
divides it into two stacked panes:

```bash
$ tmux new-session -d -s analysis -x 80 -y 24
$ tmux new-window -t analysis -n build
$ tmux split-window -t analysis:build -v
$ tmux list-windows -t analysis
0: sh- (1 panes) [80x24] [layout b25d,80x24,0,0,0] @0
1: build* (2 panes) [80x24] [layout 419a,80x24,0,0[80x12,0,0,1,80x11,0,13,2]] @1 (active)
$ tmux list-panes -t analysis:build
0: [80x12] [history 0/2000, 484 bytes] %1
1: [80x11] [history 0/2000, 452 bytes] %2 (active)
```

The `analysis:build` target names a window as
`session:window`; a pane can be addressed further as
`session:window.pane`. `list-windows` confirms two windows, the
second holding two panes, and `list-panes` shows those two panes
with their sizes. Interactively you rarely type these: `Ctrl-b
c` makes a window, `Ctrl-b "` and `Ctrl-b %` split a pane
horizontally and vertically, `Ctrl-b n`/`Ctrl-b p` move between
windows, and `Ctrl-b` with an arrow key moves between panes. For
research work you can go a long way with a single window and
never split a pane at all; the structure is there when a long
job wants its own window while you keep working in another.

## Surviving a disconnect on a remote job {.tier-beginner}

This is the payoff, and the reason the chapter exists. The
mechanism is easiest to see locally first. Start a stepwise job
in a detached session and, without ever attaching, read its
pane twice a few seconds apart:

```bash
$ tmux new-session -d -s job -x 80 -y 24 \
    "cd /tmp/ch14demo && python3 run_pipeline.py"
$ tmux ls
job: 1 windows (created Sun Jul  5 21:12:52 2026)
$ tmux capture-pane -t job -p
step 1/12 done
step 2/12 done
step 3/12 done
```

A few seconds later, a separate check, still without attaching,
shows the job has moved on by itself:

```bash
$ tmux capture-pane -t job -p
step 1/12 done
step 2/12 done
step 3/12 done
step 4/12 done
step 5/12 done
step 6/12 done
step 7/12 done
```

`capture-pane -p` prints a pane's current contents to standard
output, so you can glance at a running job without taking over
its screen. The job advanced from three steps to seven between
the two calls: it is running on its own, and the terminal only
observes it.

Now put that on a server, where it earns its keep. Let
`ch14-job.sh` stand in for a slow analysis step: a small script
that counts to thirty, printing `step N/30 done` every couple of
seconds and teeing a log (Chapter 10). The move is to start it
in a detached session over one `ssh` command, then let that
connection close, and prove the session survived by reconnecting
with *separate* `ssh` commands, each a fresh connection. Nothing
stays open across the job:

```bash
# Connection A: start the job in a DETACHED session, then return.
$ ssh lab-server 'tmux new-session -d -s job "bash ch14-job.sh"'

# Connection B (a fresh ssh): the session outlived connection A.
$ ssh lab-server 'tmux ls'
job: 1 windows (created Sun Jul  5 19:57:08 2026)

# Connection C (another fresh ssh): read the pane, no attach.
$ ssh lab-server 'tmux capture-pane -t job -p'
step 1/30 done
step 2/30 done
step 3/30 done
step 4/30 done
step 5/30 done
step 6/30 done
step 7/30 done
step 8/30 done
```

The first `ssh` returned as soon as the detached session was
created; the job kept running on the server with no connection
open. Connection C, eight steps in, was reading a job that no
one was attached to. A later, independent `ssh` finds it further
along still, and its Chapter 10 log has been written the whole
time:

```bash
# The Ch 10 log survives independently of the session:
$ ssh lab-server 'tail -3 ~/ch14-job.log'
step 14/30 done
step 15/30 done
step 16/30 done
```

Interactively this is the whole workflow: `ssh` to the server,
`tmux new -s job`, launch the job, `Ctrl-b d`, and close the
laptop. Tomorrow, `ssh` back, `tmux attach -t job`, and watch it
finish live. The connection was disposable; the session was not.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Two things persist through the disconnect, and both matter. The
*job* survives, so a run started before you left is still a run
you can trust, computed without interruption. And its *log*
survives, because the job wrote it with `tee` (Chapter 10), so
the evidence of what happened is on disk regardless of whether
you were watching. Even if the session itself ends (next
pitfall), the log remains. A result and its record both outlive
the connection, which is what lets a computation run overnight
on a server and still be believed in the morning. tmux keeps the
job alive; Chapter 10's log keeps its provenance; neither is
credited for the other's job.
:::

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Starting a session as `tmux new-session -d -s job "<command>"`
ties the session's life to that one command. By default, when
the command finishes, the pane's process exits, its window
closes, and with the last window gone the session ends, taking
the scrollback with it:

```bash
$ tmux new-session -d -s job -x 80 -y 24 \
    "echo building; sleep 2; echo done"
$ tmux ls
job: 1 windows (created Sun Jul  5 21:17:20 2026)
$ tmux ls
no server running on /tmp/tmux-1187/default
```

Come back after the job finished and the session is simply gone,
along with anything it printed. Two habits avoid the surprise:
start a session with a *shell* (`tmux new -s job`, then run the
job inside it) so the shell outlives the job and holds its
output, or, better, always write a log with `tee` (Chapter 10)
so the record survives no matter what the session does. (tmux's
`remain-on-exit` option can instead hold the finished pane open,
but the two habits above are simpler and more portable.)
:::

## What tmux does not do {.tier-advanced}

tmux is precise about what it protects, and overselling it
invites a false sense of safety. It keeps a session alive *on
the machine where it runs*, against exactly one thing: your
connection or terminal going away. It does not protect against
the machine itself going away. If the server reboots, every tmux
session on it is gone, job and scrollback with it; a session is
live processes in memory, not a saved file. This is precisely
why a long, must-not-die job on a shared cluster belongs to the
scheduler, not to tmux: you `sbatch` it (Chapter 13) so the
scheduler, not your login session, owns its lifecycle and can
requeue it under the cluster's policy, whereas tmux is for an
interactive session you personally want to detach from and
rejoin. (Surviving an actual reboot then depends on that requeue
policy and your own checkpointing, not on tmux at all.) Nor does
tmux keep a *process* alive that dies on its
own: if the job crashes or is killed, the session may stay open
(a shell is still there) but the work is over. tmux persists the
session, not the success of what runs in it.

Set against `nohup`, the distinction is now clean. Both survive
a disconnect. `nohup` is fire-and-forget: the job runs on with
its output redirected to a file, and you cannot reattach to drive
it, only watch its log grow with `tail -f` from outside. tmux
adds the three things that
make it the real workflow: you can *reattach* and watch the job
live, you can keep *several* windows and panes in one session,
and you can leave an interactive program (a REPL, an editor)
running between visits. Use `nohup` for a single fire-and-forget
command; use tmux when you will come back and look.

One quiet cost comes with that power: a detached session is easy
to forget. A session you left weeks ago is still holding a
finished job's shell, a stale environment, maybe a half-open
file, silently. `tmux ls` on login is the one-line audit; kill
what you no longer need with `tmux kill-session -t name`. The
sessions do not clean up after themselves, and on a shared
server other people can see them cluttering the process table.

## screen, and driving tmux from a script {.tier-advanced}

Before tmux there was `screen`, and it may still be the only
multiplexer on an older or minimal server where tmux is not
installed. It does the same core job, and when it is what you
have, its loop is worth recognizing:

```bash
$ screen --version
Screen version 4.09.00 (GNU) 30-Jan-22
```

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
`screen` and `tmux` solve the same problem with different keys
and different commands, so muscle memory does not transfer.
`screen`'s prefix is `Ctrl-a`, not tmux's `Ctrl-b`; you start a
named session with `screen -S work`, detach with `Ctrl-a d`,
list with `screen -ls`, and reattach with `screen -r work`. A
detached session can be started non-interactively with
`screen -dmS work <command>`. The commands differ throughout
(`screen -ls` versus `tmux ls`, `Ctrl-a d` versus `Ctrl-b d`),
so on an unfamiliar server the first question is which of the
two is installed. This book teaches tmux; `screen` is here only
so a bare server does not strand you.
:::

The same command interface that let the sandbox capture this
chapter is useful in its own right: you can drive a session from
outside it. `send-keys` types a command into a pane as if you
had typed it, and `capture-pane` reads the result back, neither
one attaching:

```bash
$ tmux new-session -d -s work -x 80 -y 24 -c /tmp/ch14demo
$ tmux send-keys -t work 'python3 run_pipeline.py' Enter
$ tmux capture-pane -t work -p
python3 run_pipeline.py
$ step 1/12 done
step 2/12 done
step 3/12 done
```

`send-keys ... Enter` sends the literal keystrokes plus a
newline, so the pane's shell runs `python3 run_pipeline.py`; the
following `capture-pane` shows the command and its first lines
of output. This is a niche tool, not part of the daily loop, but
it is how a monitoring script or a scheduled task can launch and
check on a job in a named session without a human, and it is
exactly how every tmux block in this chapter was captured.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Use the running example (`sandbox/asset-pricing/`) and a server
you can `ssh` to; if you have none yet, run the local steps in
the sandbox and read the remote one.

1. Start and detach. Run `tmux new -s build`, and inside it
   start a slow step of the pipeline (for instance `make` from
   the project root, or `python3 scripts/00_make_data.py`).
   Detach with `Ctrl-b d` and confirm with `tmux ls` that the
   session is still there.

2. Watch it without attaching. Run
   `tmux capture-pane -t build -p` twice, a few seconds apart,
   and confirm the output has advanced. Explain in one sentence
   why the job kept running while you were detached.

3. Survive a disconnect. On a server, start the same job in a
   `tmux` session over `ssh`, detach, and close the connection
   entirely. Open a new `ssh` connection, run
   `tmux attach -t build`, and confirm the job is still running.
   State in one sentence why this would have failed without tmux
   (recall Chapter 13's dropped connection).

4. Clean up, and reason about limits. End the session with
   `tmux kill-session -t build`. Then answer: if the server had
   rebooted while you were disconnected, would the job have
   survived? Why is a cluster `sbatch` job (Chapter 13) the
   right *class* of tool when the answer must be yes, and what
   does surviving an actual reboot then still depend on (recall
   the section: the cluster's requeue policy and your own
   checkpointing, not tmux)?
